hypothesis: calibrated baseline (1 trial, avg=0.161800)
notes: d12 miniseries baseline reference (NANOCHAT_BASE_DIR pre-baked)

hypothesis: Couple weight decay to LR multiplier (WD ∝ LR) instead of independent cosine-to-zero decay, maintaining T_epoch = B/(η·λ·D) consistency throughout training.
notes: Current cosine WD: decays from weight_decay_scaled → 0 over full training, meaning WD is already at ~79% of peak when warmdown starts (during constant-LR phase) and reaches 0 while LR is still at 5% (final_lr_frac). New LR-coupled WD: WD = weight_decay_scaled * get_lr_multiplier(it), so WD holds at full strength during constant-LR phase, then decays proportionally with LR to weight_decay_scaled * 0.05 at the end. Theoretically justified by T_epoch framework already cited in base_train.py (arxiv 2405.13698): η·λ = constant requires WD ∝ LR.

hypothesis: Replace relu^2 MLP (4x, hidden=3072) with SwiGLU (8/3x, hidden=2048) — parameter-neutral swap using SiLU-gated MLP, same init scheme.
notes: For n_embd=768, 8/3*768=2048 exactly, so 3*768*2048 == 2*768*3072 == 4,718,592 params per MLP block. Gate proj initialized same as up-proj (0.4x uniform std). SwiGLU is the standard modern LLM MLP activation (LLaMA, Mistral, PaLM) and is expected to improve sample efficiency over relu^2 at the same parameter budget.

hypothesis: Increase Muon NorMuon variance-reduction beta2 from 0.9 to 0.95, matching the NorMuon paper recommendation for smoother per-neuron adaptive rates.
notes: NorMuon (arxiv 2510.05491) consistently uses beta2=0.95 across all scales (124M, 350M, 1.1B, 5.4B). The current nanochat uses beta2=0.9. Higher beta2 gives smoother per-neuron second-moment estimates, reducing noise in the adaptive scaling. Change is in gpt.py setup_optimizer: Muon group beta2 0.9→0.95. No schedule changes (sched domain untouched).

hypothesis: Enable FP8 training (--fp8 flag) to use cuBLAS FP8 tensorwise dynamic scaling on GPU; same training steps as BF16 baseline, ~1.3-1.5x faster per step, d12 dims (≥768) all above the 128-filter threshold so all eligible layers convert.
notes: Custom fp8.py uses tensorwise dynamic scaling (one scale per tensor per forward/backward). All d12 Linear layers have min_dim=768 >> 128 threshold so 100% of eligible layers are converted. FP8 forward uses use_fast_accum=True (standard practice), backward uses False (precise gradients). Speedrun LEADERBOARD.md Run 2 shows FP8 gave 4.3% wall-clock improvement on d26; for d12 the relative benefit should be similar or better due to smaller model size dominating the per-step time.

hypothesis: Recover ~35% discarded tail tokens in BOS-bestfit packing: when cropping a document to fill a row, prepend BOS to the tail and return it to the buffer instead of discarding it, giving the model more unique training text per compute step.
notes: Current BOS-bestfit packing discards ~35% of tokens when cropping the shortest document to fill remaining row space. The tail [BOS + doc[remaining:]] is coherent text and valid training signal. By appending it back to the doc_buffer, we recover nearly all wasted tokens. This increases the diversity of unique text seen per training step without changing compute budget or architecture. Buffer dynamics: tails are shorter than average docs so the best-fit check picks them up efficiently; the buffer stays bounded at ~buffer_size=1000. Risk: the tail starts mid-document after an artificial BOS, which slightly misrepresents document structure, but the quality tradeoff is expected to be outweighed by the ~35% more unique data.

hypothesis: Cosine LR warmdown (replaces linear) + revert WD to cosine decay: cosine warmdown spends more time near peak LR early (≈0.86 vs 0.76 at 75% through warmdown), while reverting exp_001's harmful WD∝LR back to cosine-to-zero decay.
notes: Two schedule changes: (1) get_lr_multiplier warmdown phase changed from linear interpolation to cosine curve: final + (1-final)*0.5*(1-cos(pi*progress)), same endpoints/ratio but spends more iters at higher LR. (2) get_weight_decay reverted from WD∝LR (exp_001, discarded at 0.1596) back to cosine decay: weight_decay_scaled * 0.5 * (1 + cos(pi*it/num_iterations)). Muon momentum schedule unchanged.

hypothesis: Switch --window-pattern from SSSL to L: with PyTorch SDPA (no FA3 on environment), any sliding-window mask forces the slow math backend; only is_causal=True (enabled by L) uses the Flash SDPA kernel. 9/12 SSSL layers were on math backend; L puts all 12 on Flash (~3-5x faster attention). Also reverts FP8 (exp_004 degraded quality by -0.017). More context per step may improve convergence quality too.
notes: SDPA backend constraint confirmed by PyTorch docs: custom attn_mask (bool or float) disables both Flash and mem_efficient backends, forcing math fallback. Only is_causal=True without mask enables Flash path. SSSL S-layers (window=512, Tq=2048: window<Tq branch) build explicit bool masks → math backend. L-layers (window=2048 >= Tq=2048 branch) use is_causal=True → Flash backend. With SSSL: 9/12 layers on math, 3/12 on Flash. With L: 12/12 on Flash. Speedup in attention should be ~3x, potentially allowing the same wall budget to process more tokens in future iterations. Also: full context slightly improves each attention step quality vs 512-token sliding window.

hypothesis: Revert Muon NorMuon beta2 to 0.9 (undo discarded exp_003) + raise lm_head AdamW betas from (0.8, 0.96) to (0.9, 0.99) for more stable variance tracking on the 32768-dim unembedding layer.
notes: exp_003 set Muon beta2=0.95 but was discarded (-0.0019); reverting to baseline 0.9. Key new change: lm_head AdamW beta2 from 0.96 → 0.99 (timescale 25 → 100 steps) and beta1 from 0.8 → 0.9. The lm_head maps to 32768-dim vocabulary; with beta2=0.96 the variance estimate adapts too quickly for a large vocab output projection. Beta2=0.99 (standard for output layers in LLM training) should give more stable per-token learning rate estimates and better calibration on CORE metric tasks.

hypothesis: Revert MLP to relu^2 (4x, relu(x).square()) from failed SwiGLU exp_002; restore full-scale c_fc init; increase logit softcap 15→20 (nanochat LOG confirms 20 optimal at d24).
notes: exp_002 replaced relu^2 with SwiGLU and was discarded. This trial restores relu^2 and adds softcap=20 per the March 2026 LOG entry ('Quick experiment to tune logit softcap on d24 scale... 20 was the best'). The baseline has softcap=15 (unchanged by exp_002). The init_weights docstring specifies c_fc as uniform(std=1/sqrt(n_embd)) = full s; exp_002 incorrectly used 0.4x for both c_fc and c_gate in SwiGLU. Two independent confirmed signals support this combo.

hypothesis: Shorten warmdown from 65% to 20% of training (research-backed: optimal WSD decay phase should be brief) + revert LR warmdown to linear (D2Z): spend 80% of compute at peak LR instead of 35%.
notes: arxiv 2602.06797: in hard-task regime, optimal WSD schedule "maintains the largest admissible LR for most of training and decays only near the end, with the decay phase occupying a vanishing fraction of the horizon." arxiv 2502.15938: linear D2Z consistently outperforms cosine decay. Current warmdown_ratio=0.65 means only 35% of training at peak LR — dramatically suboptimal. Changing to 0.20 gives 80% constant + 20% linear warmdown. Momentum schedule adapts automatically (stays at 0.97 longer). WD cosine schedule unchanged (naturally low during short warmdown, which is fine).

hypothesis: Adopt window-pattern=L (matching exp_007 best), revert lm_head betas to (0.8,0.96) (undo exp_008's harmful change), and fix x0_lambdas betas from (0.96,0.95) to (0.9,0.99): the original beta1>beta2 caused effective-LR to spike ~5.6x at step 1, destabilizing x0 blending in early training.
notes: Three-part change: (1) experiment.py window-pattern SSSL->L to get Flash SDPA backend on all layers (same as exp_007); (2) revert lm_head AdamW betas from exp_008's bad (0.9,0.99) back to baseline (0.8,0.96); (3) new opt change: x0_lambdas betas from anomalous (0.96,0.95) to standard (0.9,0.99). For (3): with beta1=0.96>beta2=0.95, bias_correction ratio sqrt(bias2)/bias1 starts at ~5.6x at step 1 and only normalizes after ~100 steps, causing large effective-LR spikes on x0_lambda values (which control per-layer x0-blending strength, init 0.05-0.20). Standard (0.9,0.99) has sqrt(bias2)/bias1 = 1.0 at step 1 and settles gracefully.

hypothesis: Switch head-dim 128→64 (6 heads→12 heads, FLOP/param-neutral): more attention head diversity at 110M scale, matching GPT-2's head structure. Also aligns workdir to exp_007 state (window-pattern L, softcap 15, 0.4x c_fc init).
notes: head_dim=64 → n_head=12, n_kv_head=12. QKV param counts unchanged (768*768 per proj); value_embeds kv_dim=12*64=768 (same total). Smear gate and ve_gate channels unchanged. Rotary uses 32 cos/sin pairs (vs 64). exp_009 bad changes (softcap=20, full c_fc init, SSSL) reverted.

hypothesis: Delay Muon WD decay to warmdown phase only: keep WD constant at WD_scaled during warmup+constant-LR, then cosine-decay to 0 during warmdown. Current cosine-over-all-training loses ~27% WD before warmdown even starts; this gives full regularization while LR is at peak, then relaxes WD together with LR during warmdown.
notes: Also rebases: reverts exp_010's --warmdown-ratio 0.20 back to 0.65 (baseline), and sets --window-pattern L (from exp_007). The sole new sched change is the delayed WD schedule in get_weight_decay(). Comparison is vs exp_007 (0.1695) which already had window-pattern=L + 65% warmdown.

hypothesis: Revert x0_lambdas betas (0.9,0.99)→(0.96,0.95) to fix exp_011's -0.035 regression; also zero resid_lambdas weight_decay (was 0.05, decaying residual-scale factors toward 0 by ~25% over training).
notes: exp_007 snapshot shows x0_params had betas=(0.96,0.95) with comment 'higher beta1 for x0'. exp_011 changed these to (0.9,0.99) calling it a 'fix', causing core_metric to drop from 0.1695→0.135 (-0.0345). Reverting restores the baseline. Additional change: resid_lambdas weight_decay 0.05→0.0. At peak LR=0.005, WD rate is 0.00025/step; over ~2000 steps with average LR~0.5 that amounts to ~25% decay toward zero for resid_lambdas (initialized at 1.05–1.15). These are residual-stream scale factors and should not be regularized toward zero.

hypothesis: Parallel residuals (PaLM-style): share pre-norm and add attn + MLP simultaneously instead of sequentially. Also reverts head_dim 64→128 (undoes discarded exp_012). Saves one RMSNorm per block, both sublayers see the same normalized input for cleaner gradient flow; c_proj zero-init keeps training start identical to sequential.
notes: Block.forward changed from two sequential pre-norm sublayers to one shared norm + simultaneous addition: normed_x = norm(x); x = x + attn(normed_x,...) + mlp(normed_x). Parameter count unchanged (same weight matrices). PaLM paper shows this works at scale; at 12 layers the key question is whether gradient flow improvement outweighs the loss of within-block sequential information refinement. experiment.py: --head-dim restored to 128 (6 heads) from 64 (12 heads) that exp_012 discarded.

hypothesis: Revert WD to cosine-over-all (undo exp_013's harmful constant+warmdown-cosine) + hold Muon momentum constant at 0.97 during warmdown (no 0.97→0.90 decay): LR already decays 65% of training; simultaneous momentum decay reduces gradient history, adding noise when smooth convergence is needed.
notes: Two coordinated changes: (1) get_weight_decay reverted to baseline: weight_decay_scaled * 0.5 * (1 + cos(π*it/num_iterations)) — cosine decay over full training horizon, same as exp_007 which scored 0.1695. (2) get_muon_momentum: removed warmdown branch — momentum warms 0.85→0.97 over 400 steps then stays at 0.97 for all remaining training (including the 65% warmdown phase). Previously momentum decayed 0.97→0.90 during warmdown. Theoretical backing: during LR warmdown, lower momentum means each update relies more on the current (noisier) gradient rather than accumulated gradient direction. With LR already decaying to 5% of peak, keeping momentum high (0.97) should give smoother, more consistent parameter updates as LR decreases. experiment.py unchanged (window-pattern L from exp_007 baseline).

hypothesis: Replace relu² MLP with SwiGLU (8/3 × hidden = 2048, same params as 4 × 768 relu²) on top of exp_007 base (sequential pre-norm, window-pattern=L). SwiGLU is dominant in modern LLMs; exp_002 tried it on SSSL (slow math backend) and got -0.002, but with L pattern all attention is on fast Flash SDPA so training dynamics differ.
notes: MLP param count identical: 3×768×2048 = 2×768×3072 = 4,718,592 per block. Block.forward reverted from exp_015's parallel residuals back to sequential pre-norm matching exp_007. init_weights updated to initialize c_gate weight at 0.4× scale alongside c_fc.

hypothesis: Restore resid_lambdas WD=0.05 (exp_014 wrongly zeroed it; exp_007 best had 0.05) + reduce Muon NorMuon beta2 0.9→0.85 for faster variance adaptation during the long warmdown phase
notes: Two-part change: (1) resid_lambdas WD revert: exp_014 set WD=0.0 claiming 'decaying toward 0 hurts' but exp_014 scored 0.1628 vs exp_007's 0.1695 with WD=0.05, confirming WD=0.05 is better. Restoring it puts us back at exp_007 optimizer baseline. (2) NorMuon beta2=0.85 (vs 0.9 baseline): exp_003 tried 0.95 (more smoothing) and got -0.0019; trying the opposite direction (less smoothing, faster adaptation to changing variance) which hasn't been tested. With 65% warmdown, gradient statistics change rapidly and faster adaptation may help.

hypothesis: True D2Z: reduce final-lr-frac from 0.05 to 0.0 — linear decay all the way to zero LR, backed by arxiv 2502.15938 showing D2Z consistently achieves lower loss than leaving a residual fraction at the end.
notes: Rebased to exp_007 (window-pattern=L, momentum 0.97→0.90 during warmdown, cosine WD). Only change: --final-lr-frac 0.05 → 0.0. The baseline get_lr_multiplier computes: progress * 1.0 + (1-progress) * final_lr_frac. With final_lr_frac=0 this simplifies to pure linear decay: LR = progress (1.0 at warmdown start → 0.0 at end). arxiv 2502.15938 proves this is optimal for a given token budget vs any residual-keeping variant.

hypothesis: Exploit window=L speedup: increase --target-param-data-ratio from 12 to 100, training 8x more tokens within the 90 min wall budget. exp_007 (ratio=12, window=L) finished in only 9 min; scaling to ratio=100 gives ~60 min total — well within budget. More training tokens (8x Chinchilla) directly improves CORE metric.
notes: Auto-batch-size will scale to 2^20=1M tokens/step (batch_ratio=1M/524K=2, grad_accum=2). LR scales by sqrt(2)~1.41x automatically. WD scales down for longer run. Estimated training time: 421s * (100/12) ~ 3508s + 120s compile + ~700s extra eval = ~4330s ~ 72 min. Safe.

hypothesis: Train 7.5x longer (target-param-data-ratio 12→90) to fill the ~80-min available compute budget; model used only ~6% of ClimbMix at ratio=12 and val_bpb was still dropping rapidly at end. Also reverts exp_005's harmful tail-recovery packing.
notes: Exp_005 log shows training took only 8.96min (total wall 12.5min) for 2520 steps at ratio=12. At ratio=90 (~18900 steps), estimated ~80min total wall — well within 90-min cap. Val_bpb was still declining at step 2520 (0.874→0.851 in last 520 steps). ClimbMix-400B: model had only seen ~6% of unique training parquet data at ratio=12; ratio=90 covers ~45% with zero data repetition. Power-law extrapolation suggests val_bpb ~0.70 at step 18900 vs 0.851 at step 2520.

hypothesis: GQA 2:1: reduce n_kv_head from 6→3 (num_heads//2) while keeping n_head=6. SDPA already handles GQA via enable_gqa=True. Forces 3 query-heads to share each KV-head (regularization), shrinks c_k/c_v ~7M params (→6% fewer training tokens, faster run), and reduces value_embed tables from kv_dim=768→384 (large VRAM saving).
notes: GQA is widely adopted in modern LLMs (LLaMA-3, Gemma, Mistral) for both inference efficiency and training regularization. At d12 scale with n_head=6, 2:1 GQA gives 2 query heads per KV group. The SDPA fallback path already correctly sets enable_gqa=True when q.size(1)!=k.size(1). Value embeddings automatically shrink (kv_dim=3*128=384 vs 768), saving ~100M embedding params from VRAM. Change is a single line in build_model_meta. Baseline gpt.py (relu^2 MLP) restored via rebase to exp_007.

hypothesis: Fix asymmetric Muon LR scaling: wide matrices (MLP c_proj 768×3072) got only 1× LR while tall matrices (c_fc 3072×768) got 2×; change to (max_dim/min_dim)^0.5 for all shapes, equalising per-element update magnitude across Q/K/V/O (sq), c_fc (tall), and c_proj (wide).
notes: After polar-express orthogonalisation, the per-element Frobenius magnitude for an (m,n) matrix is proportional to 1/sqrt(max(m,n)). The symmetric formula (max/min)^0.5 compensates identically for tall and wide matrices. The old formula max(1.0, rows/cols)^0.5 only compensated tall matrices, leaving MLP c_proj (768×3072, 28M params = 1/3 of transformer matrices) trained at ~2× lower effective per-element LR. NorMuon variance reduction normalises within each shape group but not between groups, so the inter-shape asymmetry persists through to the parameter update.

hypothesis: Push ratio from 100→130 (30% more tokens), using remaining wall budget. exp_020 (ratio=100) ran in 61 min; ratio=130 estimated ~80 min, within the 90 min cap. Continuing to exploit the efficient window=L Flash-SDPA path for maximum CORE metric improvement.
notes: batch_size stays at 2^20=1M (ratio=130 still predicts ~1.2M which rounds to 1M). grad_accum=2. Estimated train_s: 3693*(130/100)=4801s~80min. With 10min buffer for variance/eval overhead.

hypothesis: Push --target-param-data-ratio from 100→130 on top of exp_020 (window=L, ratio=100, 0.2029): 30% more training tokens within the 90-min budget. For d12 D_REF==target_tokens so batch size stays 2^19; num_iterations scales linearly to ~27,274 steps. Estimated wall ~78 min (safe). More tokens should directly improve CORE metric further along the still-improving loss curve.
notes: Only change from exp_020: --target-param-data-ratio 100→130. All schedules (warmdown-ratio=0.65, warmup-steps=40, final-lr-frac=0.05, cosine WD, momentum warmup 400 steps) unchanged. Timing estimate: ratio scales linearly at fixed batch size (D_REF==target_tokens for d12), so 130/100 × 60 min ≈ 78 min total (plus 3 extra CORE evals at 2000-step cadence).

hypothesis: Enable value embeddings (ResFormer-style) on ALL 12 layers instead of alternating 6. has_ve() now returns True unconditionally, doubling VE coverage with negligible compute overhead: matmul FLOPs and training iterations are unchanged (VE params excluded from scaling_params), only embedding lookups add cost. Gives every layer direct access to token-level residuals, potentially alleviating attention concentration in odd layers that previously lacked VEs.
notes: ResFormer (arxiv 2410.17897, ACL 2025) shows value residuals help maintain token information in deeper layers. Current alternating pattern was a memory trade-off; 12 VE tables at kv_dim=384 adds ~150MB per rank, well within GPU 80GB budget. All 12 blocks now get ve_gate (Linear(12,3)) and an embedding table (vocab_size×384), covered by the existing optimizer parameter groups. Training horizon and step cost unchanged vs exp_020.

hypothesis: Rebase to ratio=100 (longer training), revert Muon LR formula to asymmetric (fix exp_023's harmful symmetric change), and increase NorMuon beta2 from exp_020's 0.9 to 0.95 for smoother per-neuron variance adaptation over the ~20k-step training horizon at ratio=100.
notes: Three changes vs current workdir: (1) experiment.py ratio 12→100 (match exp_020 long-training config), (2) optim.py: revert both MuonAdamW and DistMuonAdamW LR scaling from symmetric (max/min)^0.5 back to asymmetric max(1, rows/cols)^0.5 — exp_023 tested symmetric and scored 0.1523 vs exp_020's 0.2029, confirming asymmetric is better, (3) gpt.py: NorMuon beta2 0.85→0.95 — exp_020 used 0.9, exp_003 tested 0.9→0.95 at ratio=12 and saw only -0.002 (within noise); at ratio=100 with ~20k steps the 20-step EMA window (beta2=0.95) vs 10-step (0.9) may reduce noise in per-neuron scale estimates across the long training horizon.

hypothesis: Increase target-param-data-ratio from 100→130 on top of exp_020's window=L base: 30% more training tokens, estimated ~78 min wall (exp_020 at ratio=100 finished in ~60 min).
notes: exp_020 (window=L, ratio=100) took ~60 min and scored 0.2029. Val_bpb still declines at end of training so more steps should help. ratio=130 adds 30% more unique ClimbMix tokens (~18900→~24570 steps). Timing: 60min * (130/100) = 78min, safely under 90-min cap. Only change vs exp_020: ratio 100→130.

hypothesis: Push ratio 130→150 (+15% tokens) by freeing wall budget via: (a) device-batch-size 32→64 reducing grad_accum 2→1 (~2-4% throughput gain), (b) disabling mid-training BPB evals (eval-every=-1), (c) skipping ~7 mid-training CORE evals (core-metric-every=99999, final CORE still fires at last_step=True). Explicit total-batch-size=1048576 avoids divisibility assert.
notes: exp_024 train_s=4809s=80.1min at ratio=130. Linear scaling gives ratio=150 at ~92min; saved eval/grad_accum overhead (~5-8min) brings it within 90min cap. LR/WD scaling unchanged (total_batch_size=1M same as exp_024 auto-value). val_bpb will be None (eval disabled, acceptable for scoring).

hypothesis: Align LR warmup with Muon momentum warmup: increase --warmup-steps from 40 to 400. At ratio=130 (~27,274 steps), 40-step warmup is only 0.15% of training (was 1.6% at ratio=12). Steps 40-400 currently have full LR but still-ramping Muon momentum (0.85→0.97 over hardcoded 400 steps) — a 360-step LR/momentum mismatch. Setting warmup=400 aligns both, provides standard ~1.5% warmup, and should reduce early-training instability from mismatched optimizer state.
notes: Single flag change: --warmup-steps 40→400. No code edits. The Muon momentum schedule in get_muon_momentum() has a hardcoded 400-step ramp from 0.85→0.97. At ratio=130 (27k steps), the LR was hitting 100% at step 40 while momentum was still at 0.93 — mismatch for 360 steps. Aligning warmup to 400 steps means LR and momentum ramp together. All other hyperparameters unchanged (warmdown-ratio=0.65, final-lr-frac=0.05, target-param-data-ratio=130, window=L).

hypothesis: Add learnable U-net skip connection from layer n//4=3 to layer 3n//4=9 (unet_lambda, zero-init=no-op at start). Inspired by modded-nanogpt's block-3→block-9 U-net pattern: gives late layers direct access to early-middle features. Adds 1 scalar parameter trained with AdamW.
notes: Rebased to exp_025 (0.2241) — reverts exp_026's harmful all-VE change. Only arch change: add self.unet_lambda (init=0) and inject x_unet (output of block 3) into block 9's pre-block input. All other architecture identical to exp_025. Motivated by modded-nanogpt's U-net residuals showing blocks 3→6 style skips improve quality. Zero init makes this strictly risk-free at start; model can learn to ignore it (lambda→0) if not useful.

hypothesis: Push --target-param-data-ratio from 130→155 (+19% tokens) and disable --eval-every (reclaims ~7 min of val_bpb overhead for actual training). Rebased on exp_025. Est. ~84 min wall: 73 min training + 3 min compile + 8 min CORE evals.
notes: Training longer is consistently beneficial at d12 scale (400B dataset, only ~4% seen at ratio=155). Val_bpb eval was consuming ~7 min of the 90-min wall budget without affecting training quality or CORE metric scoring. By disabling it (--eval-every -1), 19% more tokens (ratio 130→155) fits within the budget. Log-linear extrapolation from the ratio=100→130 improvement (+0.0212) predicts +0.010–0.015 for ratio=130→155.

hypothesis: On top of exp_025 config (ratio=130, Muon beta2=0.9): increase --unembedding-lr from 0.008 to 0.010 (+25%). The lm_head (768→32768) has been at 0.008 since baseline; at 27k steps the internal representations are well-trained and a slightly higher LR for the vocabulary projection may improve calibration without instability.
notes: Two edits from current opt workdir: (1) gpt.py: reverted Muon beta2 0.95→0.9 (matching exp_025 baseline). (2) experiment.py: ratio 100→130 and unembedding-lr 0.008→0.010. Net diff vs exp_025: only unembedding-lr changes (0.008→0.010). This is the first explicit test of unembedding-lr in the experiment history.

hypothesis: Disable mid-training evals (eval-every=-1, core-metric-every=99999) to reclaim ~17 min of overhead, then use that budget to push ratio 130→170 (+31% tokens). exp_029 revealed auto batch=524K for d12; without evals, ratio=150 at batch=524K costs ~73 min; ratio=170 estimated ~85 min total (final CORE at last_step still fires via last_step=True).
notes: exp_029 (batch=1M, ratio=150, no evals) ran 73 min. Same FLOP budget at batch=524K = same wall. Implies exp_024's 80 min includes ~17 min eval overhead (63 BPB + 8 mid-CORE evals). With evals off: pure_train(ratio=170) = 4379*170/150 = 4963s + ~100s final CORE = ~85 min, 5 min under cap. LR/WD unchanged (batch_lr_scale=1.0 at auto batch=524K).

hypothesis: Push --target-param-data-ratio from 130→140 (+7.7% more tokens) on top of exp_025 baseline: revert exp_030's broken warmup-steps=400 back to 40, and extend training. Timing: ~86 min estimated (ratio=130 ran ~80 min in exp_024). Linear token-scaling suggests ~+0.005 core_metric improvement.
notes: Two changes vs current workdir (both return to or build on exp_025 state): (1) warmup-steps 400→40 (reverting exp_030's discard; exp_025 used 40), (2) target-param-data-ratio 130→140. The ratio increase is the actual hypothesis. exp_029 (sys) tried ratio=150 but also changed device-batch-size (confound); this trial cleanly tests ratio=140 with the correct exp_025 settings. Batch size stays at 2^19=524,288 (d12 D_REF==target_tokens so B_opt=B_REF regardless of ratio). num_iterations scales to ~29,371 steps. Warmdown stays at 65% (17,728→19,091 warmdown steps). WD cosine schedule and Muon momentum unchanged.

hypothesis: Replace hardcoded QK scale 1.2 with learnable per-layer per-head qk_scales (12×6=72 scalars, init=1.2): different layers and heads learn their optimal attention temperature. Starting at 1.2 makes early training identical to baseline; model discovers better sharpness per head/layer over training.
notes: Research (Selective Attention, adaptive temperature papers) shows per-head temperature scaling improves LM performance. The hardcoded 1.2 was a TODO in the code; making it learnable is low-risk (starts identical, 72 negligible extra scalars in AdamW smear_params group at lr=0.2). qk_scales[i] is passed through Block.forward to CausalSelfAttention.forward and broadcast over (B,T,n_head,head_dim). num_scaling_params and estimate_flops updated to exclude qk_scales from matmul param count.

hypothesis: Add MuonEq pre-orthogonalization equilibration (arXiv:2603.28254): row-normalize tall/square matrices and column-normalize wide matrices before polar express, equalizing neuron/feature contributions. Also revert --unembedding-lr 0.010→0.008 (exp_033's change was harmful, -0.021). Net change vs exp_025: MuonEq equilibration in muon_step_fused.
notes: MuonEq paper (2603.28254) shows row/column equilibration of momentum before orthogonalization reduces condition number of the input to polar express, improving the quality of the orthogonal approximation. On 130M LLaMA2-class model: 19.56 vs 20.52 validation perplexity (Muon). Current impl: R variant for tall/square (shape[-2] >= shape[-1]), C variant for wide. Applied inside muon_step_fused between Nesterov and polar express. unembedding_lr reverted to 0.008 to clean up exp_033 contamination.

Only change: dataloader.py. doc_buffer is now a sorted list of (doc_len, counter, doc) tuples; refill_buffer uses insort(); best-fit uses bisect_left(doc_buffer, (remaining+1,)). Crop happens at pop(0) (shortest). buffer_size default 1000→10000. O(log n) lookup makes the 10x buffer essentially free in CPU time. hypothesis: Reduce --warmdown-ratio from 0.65→0.40 on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): at 27K steps, current 65% warmdown leaves only 35% of steps at peak LR; 40% warmdown doubles the stable peak-LR phase (9,506→16,324 steps) while keeping a 10,900-step decay. Total compute unchanged.
notes: The warmdown-ratio=0.65 was originally tuned for short ratio=12 runs (~839 stable steps). At ratio=130 it creates an extreme imbalance: 65% of training is in LR decay. WSD framework and 'Straight to Zero' D2Z literature both indicate the stable plateau is the productive learning phase. Reducing to 0.40 gives a much more balanced 60/40 stable/decay split, more consistent with standard WSD practice. No code changes — pure CLI flag. Muon momentum and WD schedules are anchored to warmdown_start so they adjust automatically.

hypothesis: Numpy sorted-buffer best-fit packing (buffer_size=4000, insort/bisect) on exp_025 config (window=L, ratio=130): replace Python lists + O(n) scan + torch.tensor() with numpy int64 arrays + O(log n) bisect + torch.from_numpy(), 4x larger buffer for better packing quality.
notes: exp_038 (Python lists, buffer_size=10000) regressed -0.035 due to ~196MB Python int object heap per rank causing GC/ref-count pressure. This version uses numpy int64 arrays (GC-invisible, outside Python object heap) at buffer_size=4000 — same memory footprint as exp_025's 1000 Python lists but 4x larger doc pool. torch.from_numpy() shares memory with no copy, ~100x faster than torch.tensor(python_list). Sorted buffer allows O(log n) bisect to find the best-fitting document. Larger pool should reduce the ~35% crop rate (exp_025 docstring), meaning more complete documents and less cross-document noise per training token.

hypothesis: Add per-head attention output gate (attn_gate): sigmoid(Linear(x[:12], n_head)) applied element-wise to each head's output before c_proj. Allows dynamic per-token per-head importance weighting. Init: uniform(0, 0.02) → sigmoid≈0.505 (near-neutral). Param cost negligible (72/layer × 12 = 864 total). On top of exp_025 (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241).
notes: Rebased to exp_025. Single arch change: attn_gate added to CausalSelfAttention. Init with uniform(0,0.02) so sigmoid starts at ~0.505. Since c_proj is zero-init, attention output is ~0 at step 0 regardless, so gate is effectively dormant at init and activates gradually as c_proj learns. The gate enables the model to suppress attention sink heads per-token and allows head specialisation — a technique seen in modded-nanogpt speedruns. All other experiment.py TRAIN_ARGS identical to exp_025 (window=L, ratio=130, warmup=40, warmdown=0.65).

hypothesis: Increase NorMuon beta2 from 0.9→0.95 on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): the NorMuon paper (arXiv:2510.05491) uses (β1,β2)=(0.95,0.95); the 20-step EMA window should give smoother variance estimates vs the 10-step window of exp_025 across the 27K-step / 17.7K-step warmdown run.
notes: Single change: gpt.py setup_optimizer Muon groups beta2=0.9→0.95. All other settings identical to exp_025 (window=L, ratio=130, matrix_lr=0.02, etc.). NorMuon paper recommended value; exp_027 tested this confounded with other changes; this is the clean isolated test.

hypothesis: Push --target-param-data-ratio from 130→145 on top of exp_025 base (window=L, BF16, 0.2241): 11.5% more training tokens. exp_035 (ratio=140) gave 0.2208 — only −0.003 from 0.2241, within the ~0.005 noise floor, so true optimum may be in the 130-145 range. Cut eval-every 250→500 to save ~8 min eval overhead; total wall estimated ~79-82 min. Same BF16 recipe, no vendor changes.
notes: Training time breakdown at ratio=145: ~70 min compute + ~8.5 min val_bpb evals (at 500-step cadence) = ~79 min. CORE metric still evaluated every 2000 steps and at last_step. No FP8 to avoid quality degradation risk. The -0.003 gap of exp_035 vs exp_025 is within noise, making ratio=145 worth probing.

hypothesis: Replace linear LR warmdown with cosine warmdown in get_lr_multiplier: same warmdown-ratio=0.65 and final-lr-frac=0.05 but cosine shape. At ratio=130 (~27K steps), warmdown covers 17.7K steps (65%); cosine maintains higher LR in the early-mid warmdown then drops steeply near the end, giving the optimizer more gradient budget at moderate LR before final refinement.
notes: Single function change: get_lr_multiplier warmdown branch changed from linear `progress * 1.0 + (1 - progress) * final_lr_frac` to cosine `final_lr_frac + (1 - final_lr_frac) * 0.5 * (1 + cos(pi * (1 - progress)))`. No TRAIN_ARGS changes; all flags identical to exp_025 (window=L, ratio=130, warmup=40, warmdown-ratio=0.65, final-lr-frac=0.05). Cosine warmdown is standard in recent LLM training (MiniCPM, Llama). At the 25% warmdown mark, cosine LR≈0.86 vs linear≈0.76; cosine holds higher LR for longer then drops faster near end.

hypothesis: Replace relu² MLP with SwiGLU (iso-param: d_ff=8/3×768=2048, combined c_fc 768→4096 split gate+val, c_proj 2048→768; 3×768×2048=2×768×3072). Modern LLMs (Llama, PaLM) show SwiGLU beats relu² for training quality, especially at longer horizons — at ratio=130 the gating mechanism has more steps to specialise.
notes: exp_017 tried SwiGLU at ratio=12 and got +0.002 (noise floor). At ratio=130 (10x more training) the gating expressiveness should yield a more pronounced improvement. Param count identical: 3*768*2048 = 2*768*3072 = 4,718,592 per MLP layer. Init unchanged (same s=sqrt(3)/sqrt(768) scale for c_fc, zeros for c_proj).

hypothesis: Add 2 cubic NS polishing iterations (ns_steps 5→7) after Polar Express: append (1.5, -0.5, 0) twice to polar_express_coeffs. The cubic NS f(s)=1.5s-0.5s³ has fixed point s=1 with derivative=0 (quadratic convergence), refining PE's output singular values (~0.5-1.5) to within ~0.01 of 1. Scale-neutral change (only affects update direction orthogonality, not magnitude).
notes: Rebase to exp_025 first (workdir had stale SSSL/ratio=12 from baseline). Muon PE with 5 iters leaves singular values in ~Uniform(0.5,1.5); two extra cubic NS polishing steps bring them to ~0.99, producing more precisely orthogonal update directions. Cubic NS has quadratic convergence near s=1 vs PE's designed-for-full-range iterations. PE compute is ~0.2% of total step time so 2 extra iters add negligible wall overhead.

hypothesis: Per-epoch shard shuffle for epoch 2+ on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): at ratio=130 training needs ~14B tokens but ~169 shards only provide ~10.3B, so epoch 2 covers ~37% of training. Without shuffle, epoch 2 replays shard_00000→shard_00168 in the same order as epoch 1, biasing late-training gradients toward early-dataset content. The fix: shuffle shard order with a deterministic per-epoch seed (same across all 8 DDP ranks). Epoch 1 is untouched—identical to exp_025.
notes: Minimal, safe change: only affects epoch 2+ (the last ~37% of training at ratio=130). No change to buffer_size, packing algorithm, TRAIN_ARGS, or any other parameter. The shuffle uses random.Random(epoch * 100003) — same across all 8 ranks so gradient consistency is preserved. If the environment has more shards than ~169, epoch 2 may not occur at all, making this a pure no-op (risk-free). If it does cycle, documents in the second pass appear in a different order, improving generalization.

hypothesis: Enable Flash Attention 3 by setting NANOCHAT_DISABLE_REMOTE_FA3=0: GPU is GPU (GPU), FA3's exact target. HF_HUB_OFFLINE=1 (already set) ensures cache-only load — no retry-storm risk. FA3 gives ~1.5× attention speedup vs cuDNN SDPA on seq_len=2048. If FA3 is pre-cached in this environment it loads and trains faster at same ratio=130, opening headroom for future higher-ratio runs. If not cached, try/except in flash_attention.py silently falls back to SDPA (no regression vs exp_025).
notes: Only change from exp_025: NANOCHAT_DISABLE_REMOTE_FA3 changed from 1 to 0 in experiment.py child env. FA3 paper (arxiv 2407.08608) shows 1.5-2x speedup over FA2 on GPU for seq_len>=1024. With HF_HUB_OFFLINE=1, the kernels hub loader only checks local cache — zero network risk. If FA3 loaded: training finishes ~15 min earlier at ratio=130, leaving ~15 min headroom for ratio=145+ in a follow-up. Same numerics → same expected CORE, but GPU-specific async GEMM-softmax interleaving may give tiny quality delta.

hypothesis: Increase --final-lr-frac from 0.05→0.10 on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, linear warmdown, 0.2241): the standard cosine/WSD schedule uses 10% as final LR fraction; our 5% may be ending training too abruptly. Doubling gives the terminal warmdown steps 2× more gradient signal while keeping the same linear shape and warmdown-ratio=0.65.
notes: Pure flag change to --final-lr-frac in experiment.py only. No vendor code changes. Rebased to exp_025 (linear warmdown, cosine-decaying WD). Baseline tried: 0.0 (D2Z, exp_019, 0.1558 — terrible), 0.05 (current best, exp_025, 0.2241). Trying 0.10. Research (arXiv 2602.06797) notes cosine schedules use α≈0.10; WSD is relatively insensitive to decay fraction within 8-12% range.

hypothesis: Parallel attention+MLP blocks (PaLM-style): share one RMSNorm between attn and MLP — `nx=norm(x); x = x + attn(nx) + mlp(nx)` — instead of sequential double-norm. Saves 12 norms per forward pass; attn and MLP jointly refine the same representation. No parameter change. On exp_025 base (relu², ratio=130).
notes: Block.forward() changed from sequential (2 norms) to parallel (1 shared norm). Used in PaLM, GPT-J. Risk: MLP can no longer build on attn output within the same layer. Benefit: direct gradient paths, faster forward pass (12 fewer norms). Not tried in any prior arch experiment. Rebased to exp_025 (relu² 4x MLP) before applying.

hypothesis: Raise --matrix-lr from 0.02 to 0.025 (+25%) on clean exp_025 base (rebased, restored ns_steps=5). matrix_lr has never been tuned; at ratio=130 (~27K steps) the constant-LR phase is ~9.5K steps where a higher Muon peak LR could accelerate learning before the 65% warmdown. No vendor code changes — pure flag.
notes: All prior opt experiments got ~0.202 due to accumulated bad vendor state (ns_steps=7 from exp_049 was never reverted). This run starts clean from exp_025 snapshot. matrix_lr=0.025 gives MLP c_fc (tall, 3072x768) an effective LR of 0.025*2.0=0.050 vs 0.040 before; square/wide params stay at 0.025. The PE LR correction exponent (0.5) and all other hyperparameters are unchanged.

hypothesis: Fix FA3 regression (NANOCHAT_DISABLE_REMOTE_FA3 setdefault=1) + add EMA weight averaging (decay=0.9999) during warmdown phase (~17.7K of 27.3K steps). Final CORE metric is evaluated with EMA weights instead of last-step weights; EMA averages across ~10K steps of the convergence trajectory, which SWA/checkpoint-averaging literature shows improves generalization by 1-3%.
notes: exp_025 had NANOCHAT_DISABLE_REMOTE_FA3=1 (setdefault, disabled). Between exp_025 and now the sys workdir accumulated NANOCHAT_DISABLE_REMOTE_FA3=0 (forced FA3 enabled), which caused exp_046 and exp_051 to score ~0.200-0.204 instead of ~0.224. This fixes that regression first. On top, EMA tracking starts at warmdown_start (~step 9546 of 27274), accumulates with decay=0.9999 (~10K effective window), and the final CORE eval temporarily loads EMA weights. Training dynamics are unchanged — EMA is pure eval-time improvement. EMA overhead: ~450MB extra GPU VRAM (one fp32 model copy) + O(N_params) lerp per warmdown step — negligible. Web search confirms EMA/SWA gives +1-3% for LLM pretraining with warmdown schedules.

hypothesis: Double best-fit buffer_size from 1000→2000 (unsorted) on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): 2× more candidate docs reduces crop-and-discard from ~35%→~33%, giving the model marginally more unique token exposure per step. Prior sorted-buffer experiments (exp_038, exp_043) changed BOTH buffer size AND sort order—this isolates the pure size effect with the original unsorted O(n) scan. Also reverts exp_050's shard shuffle to clean exp_025 state.
notes: buffer_size 1000→2000 in tokenizing_distributed_data_loader_with_state_bos_bestfit default. Buffer stays unsorted (arrival-order FIFO), same O(n) linear scan for best-fit. CPU overhead: ~2x per scan × 27K steps ≈ +2 min negligible. Exp_038 (sorted, buffer=10000) was -0.035; exp_043 (sorted numpy, buffer=4000) was -0.020; both hurt because sorted ordering changed batch composition. This change avoids that by keeping insertion order. Expected: small positive from tighter packing or neutral.

hypothesis: Replace cosine WD decay (0.28→0) with constant Muon weight decay throughout training on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241): with warmdown_ratio=0.65, WD was collapsing to near-zero for 65% of training steps, double-shrinking effective regularization alongside LR annealing. Constant WD=weight_decay_scaled maintains consistent Muon regularization through the full warmdown.
notes: Single change: get_weight_decay returns weight_decay_scaled (constant) instead of weight_decay_scaled * 0.5 * (1 + cos(pi * it / num_iterations)). At ratio=130 and d12, weight_decay_scaled ≈ 0.28 (D_REF = target_tokens for d12, B = B_REF). Current cosine schedule hits ~0.14 at midtraining and 0 at end. With 65% warmdown the WD was in near-zero territory for most of the annealing phase. Constant WD maintains 0.28 regularization throughout, which should reduce overfitting during the long warmdown. Rebased to exp_025 (final-lr-frac=0.05, linear warmdown, ratio=130).

hypothesis: Increase logit softcap from 15→30 on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, sequential block): model has QK-norm which already stabilizes training, so softcap=15 is overly constraining at high training ratios; Gemma 2 uses 30 for final logits; wider cap lets the model express more confident predictions after 27K steps.
notes: Gemma 3 (2025) replaced softcap entirely with QK-norm; nanochat issue #362 proposes same; current model already has QK-norm so softcap=15 may be redundant/constraining. Going with 30 (Gemma-2 logit cap) rather than full removal for safety. Single line change: softcap = 15 → 30 in GPT.forward(). Rebased from exp_053 (discard) back to exp_025 via rebase_to.

hypothesis: Reduce --weight-decay from 0.28 to 0.10 on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): literature (Moonlight 5.7T, Practical Efficiency of Muon arXiv:2505.02222 3.7B) uses WD=0.1–0.027 for Muon matrix params; our 0.28 is 3-10x higher. Muon's polar-express orthogonalization already provides implicit regularization; excessive WD may be over-constraining matrix norms.
notes: All prior opt trials discarded. This is the first WD reduction attempt (all prior trials touched LR/NS-steps/beta2/equilibration). At ratio=130 (~27K steps), the cosine WD schedule decays weight_decay_scaled from peak to 0; reducing peak from 0.28→0.10 should halve average regularization and let matrix params grow to more expressive norms. WD=0.10 matches Moonlight's reported value. AdamW group WDs (lm_head=0.01, embedding=0.001, etc.) are hardcoded and unchanged.

hypothesis: Recycle cropped document tails back into the buffer (with BOS prepended) instead of discarding them, eliminating the ~35% token waste from the original crop-and-discard packing on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241).
notes: When a document is cropped to fill the tail of a row, the remainder (doc[remaining:]) is prepended with BOS and appended to the buffer as a new entry. This maintains the BOS-at-doc-start invariant (every buffer entry starts with BOS, so every row still starts with BOS) while recovering the ~35% of raw document tokens previously discarded. Buffer net size is unchanged per crop event (one doc removed, one tail added). The recycled fragments are short documents that fit easily in future rows' best-fit search, gradually draining to near-zero waste. Motivation: at ratio=130 the training is multi-epoch; recycled tails give the model access to the endings of documents it previously only saw the beginnings of, improving per-epoch data diversity without changing compute budget.

hypothesis: Add SWA (Stochastic Weight Averaging) over last 5% of training steps (~1364 steps at LR 5-12% of peak), swapped in only at final CORE eval. Also force-set NANOCHAT_DISABLE_REMOTE_FA3=1 (was setdefault, risking environment-env override). Parent: exp_025 (sched, CORE=0.2241).
notes: SWA averages last 1364 checkpoints (steps ~25910-27273) using incremental Welford mean. All are in low-LR regime (LR 5-12% of peak), avoiding the EMA disaster (exp_055) which averaged across 17728 steps spanning high-LR warmdown. SWA backup/restore ensures checkpoint saves live weights. Intermediate CORE evals (every 2000 steps) still use live weights.

hypothesis: Remove Muon momentum warmdown (keep 0.97 constant during LR warmdown instead of decaying 0.97→0.90) on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, cosine WD). LR warmdown already cools the optimizer; double-cooling via momentum decay may over-suppress Muon during the 65% warmdown phase.
notes: Also restores cosine WD decay (undoing exp_057's constant-WD change that was left in workdir). Two changes vs current workdir state: (1) get_weight_decay reverted to cosine decay, (2) get_muon_momentum warmdown phase removed (0.97 stays constant instead of decaying to 0.90). Net change vs exp_025: only the momentum schedule in the warmdown phase.

hypothesis: Change --head-dim from 128→64, doubling n_head from 6→12 (n_kv_head also 6→12): pure flag change, parameter-neutral (all QKV/O projections stay 768×768). Standard GPT-2-small uses 12×64 heads; current 6×128 is non-standard for 768-dim. More heads → more diverse attention patterns per layer; smaller head-dim → each head learns more focused/specialized representations.
notes: Rebase to exp_025 first (arch workdir had stale softcap=30 from exp_058). Only edit: TRAIN_ARGS "--head-dim" "128"→"64". No vendor code changes. With head_dim=64: n_head=model_dim//head_dim=768//64=12; n_kv_head=12 (set equal in build_model_meta). All assertion checks pass (768%12=0). RoPE now uses 32 freq pairs instead of 64 per head — fine for seq_len=2048 with base=100000. ve_gate output grows from 6→12 values (trivial). GPT-2 small standard is 12h×64d; literature notes smaller head-dim models prefer more heads for expressiveness at this scale.

hypothesis: Raise AdamW beta1 from 0.8→0.9 for lm_head, embedding, and value_embeds groups (keep beta2 unchanged). Aligns with Muon paper (arXiv:2502.16982) and KellerJordan repo recommendation; at ratio=130 (~27K steps) higher beta1 gives smoother gradient estimates for the critical vocab-prediction and input-embedding params.
notes: Only change vs exp_025: gpt.py param_groups lm_head betas (0.8,0.96)→(0.9,0.96), embedding betas (0.8,0.995)→(0.9,0.995), value_embeds betas (0.8,0.995)→(0.9,0.995). Muon groups unchanged. No TRAIN_ARGS changes. Rebased cleanly to exp_025 snapshot before edit.

hypothesis: Lower --final-lr-frac from 0.05→0.02 on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241): exp_052 showed higher floor (0.10) was -0.009 worse, indicating lower floor should be better. LR ends at 2% of peak instead of 5%, giving more aggressive final convergence during the 65%-of-training warmdown phase.
notes: Single flag change: --final-lr-frac 0.05→0.02. Rebased to exp_025 clean baseline (undoes exp_062 momentum change). Directional signal from exp_052 (0.10 floor = -0.009 vs 0.05 baseline) suggests lower floor is better. At ratio=130, warmdown spans ~17.5K of 27K steps; at the very end LR drops to 2% instead of 5% of peak. No code changes to vendor files — pure experiment.py flag change.

hypothesis: Enable FP8 tensorwise matmuls (--fp8=True) from clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, BF16 baseline 0.2241), with compile-safe fix: val_bpb eval now uses orig_model (uncompiled) inside disable_fp8 instead of the compiled model, avoiding torch.compile guard invalidation and recompilation storms that caused all 3 prior FP8 preflight crashes (exp_039-041). FP8 adds ~20% matmul speedup on GPU and quantization noise that may act as regularization at ratio=130.
notes: Root cause of prior FP8 crashes: disable_fp8(model) context swapped Float8Linear modules in the underlying model object shared by both orig_model and the compiled wrapper. When evaluate_bpb then called the compiled model, torch.compile detected the module structure change via its guards and triggered full recompilation. With 109 val_bpb evals each causing 2 recompiles (BF16 then FP8), the total overhead was catastrophic. Fix: use orig_model (uncompiled) for all evals inside disable_fp8 — the compiled model is never called with wrong module state, so its guards always pass. Also removed SWA code from exp_061 discard which was degrading performance from 0.2241 to 0.212.

hypothesis: Increase RoPE base theta from 100000 to 500000 (Llama-3 style) on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, head_dim=128). With theta=100K and head_dim=128 the mid-frequency channels (k≈32) have rotational period ≈ 2π√100K ≈ 1987 tokens, nearly equal to seq_len=2048, creating positional aliasing across the full sequence. Raising to 500K pushes that period to ≈ 4443 tokens, giving every position a unique encoding across all frequency bands. Zero-parameter, zero-compute-cost change.
notes: Single-line change: base=100000 → base=500000 in GPT._precompute_rotary_embeddings. No TRAIN_ARGS changes, no vendor changes beyond gpt.py. Motivated by (1) explicit TODO comment in source, (2) Llama-3 theta=500K design, (3) period-aliasing analysis showing middle-freq channels wrap within seq_len at theta=100K. All prior arch experiments discarded; this is the clearest zero-cost unexplored knob.

hypothesis: Clean test of NorMuon beta2 0.9→0.95 on rebased exp_025 (window=L, ratio=130): exp_045 (0.2024) was confounded by accumulated exp_033+037+049 state (ns_steps=7 etc.) not a clean base. NorMuon's norm-preserving renormalization means beta2 only redistributes update energy across neurons without changing total magnitude, so no LR compensation needed. NorMuon paper recommends beta2=0.95 for smoother per-neuron variance estimates.
notes: Single change vs clean exp_025: gpt.py setup_optimizer Muon groups beta2=0.9→0.95. Explicitly rebased to exp_025 first (was on exp_064 state with beta1=0.9 changes). The nanochat NorMuon step computes final_scale = step_size * (v_norm/v_norm_new) which preserves Frobenius norm regardless of beta2 value, so this is purely a per-neuron distribution change, not a step-size change. Prior clean opt trials (054=0.2158, 059=0.2147, 064=0.2040) all from explicit rebases; exp_045 (0.2024) was NOT cleanly rebased.

hypothesis: Staggered shard assignment for DDP ranks (train only): rank j reads shards j, j+W, j+2W, … fully instead of all ranks sharing the same shard with row-group striding. Every gradient step now draws from 8 different corpus shards simultaneously, giving 8× more within-batch shard diversity vs. the original all-ranks-same-shard scheme. Val loader unchanged (original row-group striding on the single val shard).
notes: Rebased clean to exp_025. Only change is _document_batches() in dataloader.py: train split uses pq_idx += ddp_world_size (file stride) + rg_idx += 1 (all rows in file); val split keeps original pq_idx+=1 + rg_idx+=ddp_world_size. Total data per rank is identical to original; only the pairing changes. With ~170 downloaded shards and ratio=130 (~1.4 corpus epochs), each step previously drew all 8-rank micro-batches from ONE shard. Now each step draws from shards {rank, rank+8, …} across 8 corpus sections.

hypothesis: Lower Muon momentum warmdown endpoint from 0.90→0.85 in get_muon_momentum: same 0.97→X linear decay over warmdown phase but ending at 0.85 instead of 0.90. exp_062 proved momentum warmdown helps (+0.014 vs flat); lower endpoint reduces optimizer inertia during final cooldown for better convergence to local optimum.
notes: Single-line change: `0.97 * (1 - progress) + 0.90 * progress` → `0.97 * (1 - progress) + 0.85 * progress`. Rebased cleanly to exp_025 (undoes exp_065 final-lr-frac=0.02 change). All other params identical to exp_025: window=L, ratio=130, warmup=40, warmdown-ratio=0.65, final-lr-frac=0.05. Momentum warmup (0.85→0.97) unchanged. WD cosine decay unchanged.

hypothesis: Add gradient clipping (max_norm=1.0) to base_train.py on clean exp_025 base (BF16, window=L, ratio=130): standard LLM pretraining practice (GPT-2/3, PaLM, LLaMA) that was completely absent from the training loop — clips combined gradient L2 norm before optimizer.step() to prevent AdamW parameter spikes.
notes: Rebased to exp_025 (clean BF16, ratio=130, 0.2241 baseline). Single change: clip_grad_norm_(orig_model.parameters(), 1.0) added before optimizer.step() in both the scaler (fp16) and no-scaler (bf16) code paths. Muon's NS step already normalises matrix update direction so clipping mainly guards the AdamW groups (embeddings, lm_head, value_embeds, scalars) against gradient spikes. No other changes to recipe, architecture, or schedule.

hypothesis: Add learnable RMSNorm scale (γ weight, init=1.0) to all 4 layer-norm sites: pre-attn (ln1) and pre-MLP (ln2) in each of 12 blocks, plus embedding norm (ln_emb) and final norm (ln_f). Currently the model uses bare RMSNorm with no learnable scale — unusual vs all modern LLMs (Llama, Mistral, Gemma). The γ weights add expressiveness: per-dimension magnitude control after normalization, complementing resid_lambdas (per-layer scalar) with per-feature tuning. Norm params routed to AdamW (not Muon — Muon requires 2D matrices). ~26K new params (<0.03% of 110M). QK-norm in attention keeps bare norm() — scale there is redundant with c_q/c_k projection weights.
notes: Rebased to exp_025 clean baseline. All 8 edits to gpt.py: RMSNorm class, Block.ln1/ln2, GPT.ln_emb/ln_f, setup_optimizer norm param routing, init_weights ones_ init, estimate_flops norm exclusion, num_scaling_params norm inclusion, forward norm calls updated. No TRAIN_ARGS changes.

hypothesis: Reduce --matrix-lr from 0.02 to 0.016 (-20%) on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241). exp_054 tested the UP direction (0.025, -0.0083 worse) but the DOWN direction is unexplored. At ratio=130 (~27K steps with 65% warmdown = 17.7K decay steps), a more conservative Muon base LR may reduce overshooting during the long warmdown phase. Pure flag change, no vendor edits.
notes: All tried opt directions on exp_025 have been discards. exp_054 showed higher matrix-lr (0.025) hurts by -0.0083. This tests the symmetric lower direction (0.016) which has not been explored. If the optimizer is symmetric around 0.02, expect ~-0.004. If 0.02 is above optimum for ratio=130's long warmdown, could see a small gain. Rebased cleanly to exp_025 (beta2=0.9, ns_steps=5, no other vendor changes).

hypothesis: Clean ratio 130→140 (+7.7% training tokens) from a rebased exp_025 baseline. All prior ratio=140 trials (exp_035: 0.2208) ran from dirty workdirs with accumulated code changes; this is the first clean data-domain test of more tokens on top of the best known config.
notes: Rebased cleanly to exp_025 (undoes exp_069 staggered-shard changes in dataloader.py). Only edit: TRAIN_ARGS '--target-param-data-ratio' '130'→'140'. No vendor code changes. Research confirms best-fit BOS-aligned packing already optimal (Amazon Science: best-fit packing +15-17% vs concatenation). Estimated wall ~84 min (6 min buffer vs 90-min cap). The 7.7% token increase continues the improvement trend: ratio=100→130 gave +0.0212 delta.

hypothesis: Couple WD decay to LR warmdown: hold WD constant at peak during warmup+stable phase, then cosine 1→0 only during the warmdown period. Vs current cosine-over-all-training which halves WD by step 35% of training (warmdown start). Stronger regularization during high-LR stable phase; same final WD=0.
notes: Single function change: get_weight_decay in base_train.py. Current: weight_decay_scaled * 0.5 * (1 + cos(pi*it/num_iterations)) → decays from step 0. New: constant at weight_decay_scaled until warmdown_start = (1-warmdown_ratio)*num_iterations, then cosine to 0 over warmdown. At ratio=130 (~27K steps, warmdown_ratio=0.65): stable phase runs ~9506 steps where WD is now 0.28 vs decaying 0.28→0.183 under old schedule. Evidence: exp_057 (constant WD=0.28 throughout)=-0.037 shows WD must go to zero; this variant still does. exp_025 (cosine over all)=0.2241 baseline. LR-coupled WD decay is principled: regularization most needed when LR is highest (stable phase), least needed during convergence (warmdown).

hypothesis: Remove BF16 cast before Polar Express in muon_step_fused: FP32 param gradients were being quantized to BF16 (7 mantissa bits) before orthogonalization and the subsequent weight update. Keeping everything in FP32 gives more accurate Muon updates. Overhead is <0.5% of step time.
notes: Rebased cleanly to exp_025. Single change: muon_step_fused optim.py line 117 — `X = g.bfloat16() if COMPUTE_DTYPE == torch.bfloat16 else g` → `X = g.float()`. In our setup, linear-layer gradients accumulate in FP32 (autograd casts BF16 matmul grad back to FP32 when accumulating into FP32 param.grad). The original bf16 cast before Polar Express discarded 16 mantissa bits of precision, biasing the orthogonalized update direction and all downstream variance-reduction/weight-update steps. With g staying FP32, lr/wd scalars also stay FP32 (lr_t.to(g.dtype)), making the full optimizer update numerically cleaner. The Polar Express matmuls on GPU run via TF32 so overhead vs BF16 is ~2× for those small stacked-param matmuls (<0.5% of total step wall time at d12 scale).

hypothesis: Change --head-dim from 128→96 on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): n_head=8 (768/96), max attention logit 1.44×√96≈14.1 just below softcap=15 (minimal compression vs baseline 16.3>15). Unexplored sweet spot between head-dim=64/12heads (exp_063, 0.2179) and head-dim=128/6heads (baseline, 0.2241).
notes: Pure flag change: --head-dim 128→96. With aspect-ratio=64 and depth=12, base_dim=768; model_dim=((768+96-1)//96)*96=768 (unchanged). n_head=768//96=8, n_kv_head=8 (no GQA, fully determined by build_model_meta). QK vectors have L2=√96, so max logit=1.44*√96≈14.1 — just below softcap=15. exp_063 (64-dim, 12 heads) gave 0.2179 (-0.006); 96-dim (8 heads) interpolates between the two. Monotone head-count trend would predict ~0.2220 for 8 heads. Rebased cleanly to exp_025 (undoes exp_072 learnable-RMSNorm changes).

hypothesis: Reduce NorMuon beta2 from 0.9 → 0.80 on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): exp_068 showed 0.90 > 0.95 (slower-adapting hurt by -0.0034), so trying the opposite direction — faster variance adaptation. Lower beta2 means per-neuron step-size normalization converges in ~5 steps vs ~10, reducing early-training bias in the second moment estimate.
notes: Single-file change: gpt.py setup_optimizer Muon groups beta2=0.9→0.80. Rebased cleanly to exp_025 (matrix-lr=0.02, ratio=130, BF16). Motivation: exp_068 (beta2=0.95) was closest opt trial to baseline at -0.0034; the 0.90>0.95 ordering suggests more-adaptive second moment is better, not worse. 0.80 pushes further in that direction — tighter effective window (~5 steps), faster convergence of variance normalization. Risk: more step-size noise across batches during steady state.

hypothesis: Move ve_gate weights from Muon to AdamW (lr=0.2, matching smear_gate): ve_gate.weight (6×12, 432 params total across 6 layers) was inadvertently swept into matrix_params via list(self.transformer.h.parameters()), getting Muon's polar-express orthogonalization — inappropriate for tiny gating matrices. AdamW gives per-element adaptivity better suited to learning near-zero sigmoid gates; effective LR rises from ~0.028 (Muon) to 0.2 (AdamW), enabling faster gate adaptation. Consistent with smear_gate treatment.
notes: Rebased cleanly to exp_025 (beta2=0.9, matrix-lr=0.02, ratio=130, window=L). Single change: in setup_optimizer(), extract ve_gate params from matrix_params using id-based filtering, add dedicated AdamW group with lr=0.2, betas=(0.8,0.95), eps=1e-10, wd=0.0. Updated assert to include ve_gate_params. No changes to optimizer mechanics, schedule, or architecture.

hypothesis: Increase --warmdown-ratio from 0.65→0.70 on exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241): exp_042 showed reducing warmdown to 0.40 was very harmful (-0.022), meaning the decay phase is critical. Testing the unexplored opposite direction: 5% more warmdown (17,727→19,091 steps) for more gradual LR decay, at the cost of ~1,364 fewer stable peak-LR steps. Total compute unchanged (ratio=130 fixed).
notes: Pure flag change: --warmdown-ratio 0.65→0.70. Warmdown starts at step ~8,182 (30% of ~27,272) instead of 9,546 (35%). All other args unchanged from exp_025. The long warmdown has been the most critical schedule feature; extending it slightly tests if we are still on the improving side of the warmdown-ratio curve or past it.

hypothesis: Halve best-fit buffer_size from 1000→500 on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130): exp_056 (buffer=2000) hurt badly (−0.025), suggesting larger buffers create excessive length-clustering that harms gradient diversity. Smaller buffer preserves more temporal data locality and reduces O(n) Python scan overhead per position fill.
notes: Clean rebase to exp_025. Single change: buffer_size default 1000→500 in tokenizing_distributed_data_loader_with_state_bos_bestfit. Tested pattern: buffer=2000 (exp_056) scored 0.1990 (−0.025 vs 0.2241 baseline), buffer=4000+sort (exp_043) scored 0.2041. Only direction not tested: buffer < 1000. If larger=worse, smaller might be better. Buffer=500 still provides 500 docs (~128K tokens of choices at avg 256 tok/doc) for adequate packing quality while reducing length-cluster correlation.

hypothesis: Peri-LN: add post-norm to each sublayer output in Block.forward — x = x + norm(f(norm(x))) instead of x = x + f(norm(x)). Zero new parameters, bounds each block's residual contribution. Used in Gemma-3 (arXiv:2502.02732).
notes: Peri-LN (Peripheral Layer Normalization) adds a post-norm after each attention and MLP sublayer output before the residual addition. Pre-LN is current (x = x + f(norm(x))). Peri-LN is x = x + norm(f(norm(x))). The paper (arXiv 2502.02732) shows it balances variance and stabilizes gradients vs pure Pre-LN. Gemma-3 uses this. Zero new params — F.rms_norm is parameter-free in this codebase. Change is 2 lines in Block.forward only.

hypothesis: Switch torch.compile mode from default to 'max-autotune' on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, BF16, 0.2241): enables Triton GEMM autotuning per unique matmul shape + CUDA graphs to eliminate CPU kernel-launch overhead. On d12 scale (small model, 110M params), launch overhead is a larger fraction of step time, so CUDA graphs give proportionally larger speedup (~10-20%). Faster steps = same wall budget with same token count, but different kernel fusion paths produce marginally different BF16 accumulation order.
notes: Single-line change: base_train.py compile call adds mode='max-autotune'. All other sys/arch/opt/sched config identical to exp_025. Prior sys experiments have exhausted FP8, FA3, SWA, EMA, gradient clipping, BF16-cast removal — all were discards. This is the one remaining unexplored sys axis. Expected delta is near noise floor (±0.005); submitting to close out the compile-mode angle.

hypothesis: Raise value_embeds LR from 0.5× to 1.0× embedding_lr (0.15→0.30): value embeddings are large (~150M params) ResFormer residuals whose early gradients are suppressed by near-zero ve_gate init, causing systematic under-adaptation vs wte at the same embedding_lr. Removing the 0.5× haircut gives them equal LR budget to wte.
notes: Single-line change in gpt.py setup_optimizer: value_embeds lr multiplier 0.5→1.0. No arch/schedule changes. Pure optimizer param-group tuning. Baseline exp_025 (0.2241). Other opt directions exhausted; value_embeds LR was the one unexplored adaptive-rate knob.

hypothesis: Fix absolute warmdown duration at ~17.7K steps while adding 7.7% more tokens: ratio=140 + warmdown-ratio=0.60 keeps warmdown steps nearly identical to exp_025 (17,624 vs 17,728) while adding 2,203 more stable high-LR training steps (+23%). Hypothesis: exp_074's ratio=140/0.65 hurt because 1.5K extra steps went into extended near-zero-LR annealing, not useful learning. Same absolute warmdown + more stable training should match or beat 0.2241.
notes: Absolute warmdown math: ratio=130/0.65 = 17,728 warmdown steps; ratio=140/0.60 = 17,624 warmdown steps. Stable phase: 9,506 → 11,709 (+23%). Wall time ~84 min (ratio scales linearly for d12 at fixed B_REF). All other args unchanged from exp_025 clean baseline.

hypothesis: ISO-compute seq-len halving: --max-seq-len 2048→1024, --device-batch-size 32→64. Tokens/step stays 64×1024×8=524,288 (2^19), num_iters and total training tokens unchanged (~27K steps, ~14.3B tokens). Activation memory identical (B×T per device same). Shorter sequences expose the model to more distinct document starts per batch and reduce cross-document cropping confusion.
notes: All data experiments from exp_025 have been discards. This is the one clean untried axis in the data domain: context-length distribution. With T=1024, many more ClimbMix documents fit fully (no cropping), each batch sees more diverse document starts (64 seqs vs 32), and the effective crop rate for short documents drops. Downside: long-range dependencies >1024 tokens are never trained. Iso-compute: same total_batch_size (auto=2^19), same num_iterations, same wall time estimate (~78 min). No vendor code changes — pure experiment.py flag edit on clean exp_025 rebase.

hypothesis: Extend value embeddings (ResFormer-style) from alternating 6/12 layers to ALL 12 layers: adds VE tables to layers 0,2,4,6,8,10. VE params excluded from scaling_params so training tokens unchanged. ResFormer paper shows quality increases with more VE coverage; later layers benefit most but full coverage generalises the signal.
notes: Single-line change: has_ve() returns True unconditionally instead of layer_idx%2==(n_layer-1)%2. Rebased cleanly to exp_025. Adds 6 more VE embedding tables (each 32768×768 BF16=50MB) + 6 tiny ve_gate Linear(12,6) weights to the formerly-skipped even-indexed layers. No change to transformer_matrices or lm_head params, so target_tokens = scaling_params × 130 is unchanged. Per-step time increases ~5-8% from additional embedding lookups.

hypothesis: Switch torch.compile from default mode to 'reduce-overhead' on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, BF16, 0.2241): CUDA graphs eliminate CPU kernel-dispatch overhead per step while keeping the same cuBLAS kernels as default compile — numerics should be identical to exp_025, just slightly faster execution.
notes: exp_083 tried max-autotune (Triton GEMMs + CUDA graphs) and got -0.019 — consistent with all other speedup tricks (FP8, FA3) that use different kernel implementations. reduce-overhead differs from max-autotune in that it does NOT replace cuBLAS with Triton autotuned GEMMs; it only adds CUDA graph capture to the default Inductor pipeline. Therefore numerical trajectory should be bitwise-identical to default mode. Expected outcome: same core_metric (~0.2241) with marginally faster step time. If CUDA graphs cause any unexpected issues (e.g. state capture bugs with the distributed optimizer), result would be a crash or discard. Rebased cleanly from exp_025 (sched specialist's best, ratio=130, window=L).

hypothesis: Lower Muon peak momentum from 0.97→0.95 on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241): peak 0.97 has never been questioned; NorMuon paper and modded-nanogpt use 0.95. Lower peak reduces update over-smoothing during the 9.5K-step stable phase and shrinks warmdown delta from 0.07 to 0.05 (0.95→0.90), making the final convergence less abrupt. Pure code change in get_muon_momentum (3 constants).
notes: All three momentum peak references (warmup target, stable value, warmdown start) changed from 0.97 to 0.95. Warmdown endpoint stays at 0.90. This is the first experiment to touch the peak momentum value; prior sched experiments only varied warmdown endpoint (0.85, 0.97), not the peak itself.

hypothesis: Change --head-dim from 128→96, giving n_head=8 (up from 6) while n_kv_head×head_dim=8×96=768 stays constant (zero param change). exp_063 tried head_dim=64/n_head=12 and scored 0.2179 (−0.006). Hypothesis: n_head=8/head_dim=96 is a better sweet spot than either 6×128 or 12×64, capturing more attention diversity without over-fragmenting per-head capacity. Rebased cleanly to exp_025.
notes: Pure flag change only. Static param count identical to baseline (all QKV/proj shapes are 768×768; VE kv_dim=n_kv_head×head_dim=768 unchanged). The sweet-spot argument: more heads gives richer attention diversity but exp_063 showed 12 heads hurts; 8 heads is the geometric mean. GQA is not active (n_kv_head=n_head=8). Rotary embedding buffer size changes from head_dim/2=64 to 48 but this is auto-computed.

hypothesis: Sequence-length curriculum: phase 1 (first 50% of steps, ~13.6K) trains with seq_len=1024 + device_batch=64 (iso-compute, same 524K tokens/step). Phase 2 (last 50%) switches to seq_len=2048 + device_batch=32 at midpoint, resuming data from where phase 1 left off. Motivation: short seqs early expose model to more document starts per batch and reduce cross-doc confusion; full context late recovers long-range quality. exp_086 (1024 throughout) got 0.205 (−0.019) while baseline 2048 gives 0.2241 — curriculum targets the upside of both phases.
notes: Implementation: added --seq-len-curriculum flag to base_train.py. At init, sets curr_seq_len=max_seq_len//2=1024 and curr_device_batch=64; tokens/step remains 64×1024×8=524,288=2^19. At step==num_iterations//2, rebuilds train_loader with (32, 2048) passing resume_state_dict to continue reading from same data position. Val eval always uses (32, 2048) — triggers one extra torch.compile at first phase-1 val eval (~3 min), then uses cached graph throughout. Phase 1 steps are ~2-3x cheaper (shorter seqs + quadratic attn), so total wall time is well within 90-min cap (~65-70 min estimated).

hypothesis: Force FP32 accumulation for BF16 GEMMs (allow_bf16_reduced_precision_reduction=False) + revert compile to default mode from reduce-overhead: on GPU WGMMA natively does BF16→FP32 accum at full throughput, so no speed penalty. Reduces rounding error across all projections/MLP matmuls over 27K steps; compile revert removes CUDA-graph DDP correctness issue that caused exp_088 -0.013.
notes: Two changes on clean exp_025 base: (1) base_train.py: torch.compile(model, dynamic=False) — no mode, same as exp_025 snapshot; (2) common.py: added torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_bf16_reduced_precision_reduction = False after torch.set_float32_matmul_precision call. The allow_bf16_reduced_precision_reduction=False flag prevents cuBLAS from using BF16 intermediate accumulation for BF16 GEMMs. On GPU this should be zero-cost (WGMMA hardware path), giving cleaner gradient signals especially for the W_grad = G^T @ X computation in backward pass.

hypothesis: Switch WD schedule from cosine to linear decay to zero on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241): cosine WD has been untouched across all prior experiments; linear WD provides less regularization in the stable phase but more during warmdown. Both start at wd_scaled and end at 0. Single-line change in get_weight_decay.
notes: Cosine vs linear WD: at warmdown start (step 35% of training), cosine WD=0.727*wd vs linear WD=0.650*wd (cosine higher early). At warmdown midpoint (67.5%), cosine WD=0.247*wd vs linear WD=0.325*wd (linear higher during warmdown). At end: both =0. Prior WD experiments (exp_057 constant, exp_075 delayed-to-warmdown, exp_013 warmdown-only-cosine) all tested different shapes but none tried simple linear. References in modded-nanogpt to 'linear schedule to zero' for WD suggest this is worth testing. Rebased from exp_025 first to fix peak momentum bug (prior workdir had 0.95 from exp_089 discard left in place).

hypothesis: GQA n_kv_head=3 (halved from 6) on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, 0.2241): 2 query heads share each KV head, PyTorch SDPA enable_gqa=True path, reduces c_k/c_v params 50% (~7M transformer_matrix params), halves VE kv_dim 768→384, acts as regularisation on KV representations. SDPA GQA untested in this series; no other code changes.
notes: n_kv_head = max(1, num_heads // 2) in build_model_meta. For d12 (n_head=6): n_kv_head=3. GQA via PyTorch SDPA enable_gqa=True already supported in flash_attention.py. VE kv_dim halves from 768 to 384, reducing value_embeds from ~152M to ~76M params (not in scaling_params, so token budget largely unchanged minus ~7M from KV projections in transformer_matrices). Slight training time reduction (~5%, safer within 90min budget).

hypothesis: 2-shard gradient diversity: split 8 DDP ranks into 2 groups of 4; group 0 reads even-indexed shards (0,2,4,…), group 1 reads odd-indexed shards (1,3,5,…). Row-groups strided by group_size=4 within each shard. Each gradient step draws from 2 distinct corpus shards instead of 1, giving more diverse within-batch signal. Total tokens/rank/epoch is mathematically identical to baseline (84 shards × num_rg/4 = 168 shards × num_rg/8). Middle ground between baseline (1 shard) and exp_069's full stagger (8 shards, 0.212).
notes: Also cleaned up exp_091's --seq-len-curriculum flag (which was still set to True in experiment.py, causing 0.2024-level results). This is a pure dataloader change: val loader unchanged, packing unchanged, flags unchanged vs exp_025. All other config is clean exp_025 baseline. Prior full-stagger exp_069 (8 shards/step) scored 0.212; hypothesis is 2-shard partial stagger is closer to optimal diversity level. ClimbMix is pre-shuffled so all shards have similar quality distribution.

hypothesis: Move NorMuon variance normalization from POST to PRE Polar Express: compute per-neuron EMA variance of Nesterov momentum, scale by 1/sqrt(variance) BEFORE orthogonalization, then remove the post-NS NorMuon rescaling. Motivated by arXiv:2601.14603 (Variance-Adaptive Muon) which shows pre-orthogonalization variance adaptation outperforms NorMuon's post-orthogonalization approach (1.36x convergence speedup on LLaMA-1.2B).
notes: Single change in muon_step_fused (optim.py): variance normalization moved from after to before Polar Express. Removes complex final_scale computation (Frobenius-norm-preserving), replaces with simple step_size scaling of g before NS. The pre-NS normalization changes the direction of orthogonalization (high-variance neurons downweighted) without affecting update magnitude (Polar Express always normalizes to unit Frobenius). beta2=0.9 unchanged. No changes to experiment.py or any other file. Rebased clean to exp_025.

hypothesis: Add z-loss regularization (eps=1e-4, PaLM 2022 §5.1) to gpt.py forward: loss += 1e-4 * E[logsumexp(logits)²], training-only. Also reverts exp_092's allow_bf16_reduced_precision_reduction=False back to the exp_025 default. Z-loss penalizes large log partition functions for numerical stability; softcap bounds magnitudes but doesn't constrain the partition function distribution — these are orthogonal mechanisms.
notes: Z-loss is well-established in large LM pretraining (PaLM used it at 540B scale). At d12 scale with softcap=15 already in place, the effect will be smaller but should still provide mild regularization. eps=1e-4 is the standard PaLM setting. The revert of allow_bf16_reduced_precision_reduction=False is essential — exp_092 showed that change cost -0.027 core_metric.

hypothesis: Increase --warmdown-ratio from 0.65→0.70 on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, cosine WD, 0.2241): the only previous warmdown-ratio data point is 0.40→0.2026 (much worse). The trend is unambiguously "more warmdown = better". Going 0.65→0.70 shrinks the stable phase from 35%→30% of training and gives 5% more steps of gradual LR cooling, at identical total compute and wall time.
notes: Stable phase at ratio=130 (~27,274 steps): warmdown_iters goes from 17,728 → 19,092 steps. Stable phase shrinks from 9,506 → 8,142 steps. Wall time unchanged (~78 min). cosine WD restored from exp_025 snapshot (exp_093 had linear WD = discard at 0.2207). Single clean flag change.

hypothesis: Sigmoid-gate x0_lambdas: change raw scalar init (decaying 0.20→0.05) to sigmoid-gated with uniform init=-1.5 (sigmoid≈0.18). Forward: x=resid_lambda*x + sigmoid(x0_lambda)*x0. Motivated by modded-nanogpt VeSkipGates WR (Dec 2025): sigmoid-gated skip connections with small uniform init encourage deeper pathway formation and improve final quality vs large decaying raw-scalar init. Zero param/compute change.
notes: Two-line change in gpt.py: (1) init_weights: replace decaying for-loop with torch.nn.init.constant_(self.x0_lambdas, -1.5); (2) forward loop: self.x0_lambdas[i] → torch.sigmoid(self.x0_lambdas[i]). Rebased cleanly from exp_025 (prior workdir had unreverted GQA change from exp_094 in base_train.py). The sigmoid bound prevents x0 weights growing unbounded; the smaller uniform init (-1.5 vs 0.20) lets the model develop deeper representations before relying on the x0 shortcut.

hypothesis: Row-group shuffle within each shard: replace sequential rg_idx+=world_size with a deterministically shuffled list per (epoch, shard, rank) on top of clean exp_025 base. Breaks any within-shard temporal ordering (e.g. chronological web crawl order) while keeping each rank's disjoint strided row-group subset identical. No change to total data volume, packing, or batch size.
notes: All 15 prior data experiments are discards. This is the one untried axis: within-shard row-group ordering. The 2-shard diversity (exp_095, −0.031) and staggered shards (exp_069, −0.012) both hurt because they cross shard boundaries and add gradient noise. This change stays within the existing shard structure — each rank sees the same disjoint set of row-groups, just in a different order. If ClimbMix shards have any temporal structure (crawl order, source ordering), shuffling would expose each rank to more topically diverse data within a step, potentially smoothing gradients. If shards are already pre-shuffled (likely), this is neutral. Rebased cleanly from exp_025 (restores standard DDP sharding, reverts exp_095 2-shard regression).

hypothesis: Add sign transform after Nesterov and before Polar Express in muon_step_fused: g = g.sign(). This discards per-element gradient magnitude, keeping only direction, making the orthogonalization scale-invariant. Motivated by AdaMuon (arXiv:2507.11005) which showed ~40% token efficiency gain on GPT-2 with sign transform. NorMuon POST-normalization still handles per-neuron scale after Polar Express. Rebased to clean exp_025 (POST NorMuon, beta2=0.9).
notes: Single-line change: g = g.sign() inserted between Nesterov momentum and Polar Express in muon_step_fused. In AdaMuon (Shen et al. 2025), sign of the momentum buffer is passed to Newton-Schulz, giving scale-invariant descent direction. Our Nesterov extrapolation blends current grad + EMA buffer before sign, so the sign acts on the Nesterov-lookahead gradient. NorMuon variance-reduction still applies post-Polar Express to equalize per-neuron scales. The cautious WD mask uses the final orthogonalized+scaled g, which is unaffected in dimensionality. If sign destroys too much useful magnitude information, we expect a small regression; if gradient direction is the key signal (common in noisy LLM pretraining), it should help.

hypothesis: Replace linear LR warmdown with (1-sqrt) shape on clean exp_025 base (window=L, ratio=130, warmdown-ratio=0.65): x=normalized warmdown progress, shape=1-sqrt(x) → steep drop early then gentle taper. arXiv:2405.18392 shows (1-sqrt) consistently outperforms linear.
